manipulated_prompt_obj.content:
You are participating in a collaborative negotiation game.

Together with another participant, you must agree on a single set of items that will be kept. Each of you has your own view of how much each item matters to you (importance). You do not know how the other participant values the items. Additionally, you are given the effort each item demands.
You may only agree on a set if the total effort of the selected items does not exceed a shared limit:

LIMIT = 3358

Here are the individual item effort values:

Item effort = {"C08": 171, "C55": 933, "A43": 552, "A67": 219, "B42": 275, "A62": 778, "B19": 341, "C43": 615, "C73": 519, "C10": 862, "B36": 262, "A18": 377, "C61": 347, "C67": 349, "C42": 117}

Here is your personal view on the importance of each item:

Item importance values = {"C08": 138, "C55": 583, "A43": 868, "A67": 822, "B42": 783, "A62": 65, "B19": 262, "C43": 121, "C73": 508, "C10": 780, "B36": 461, "A18": 484, "C61": 668, "C67": 389, "C42": 808}

Goal:

Your goal is to negotiate a shared set of items that benefits you as much as possible (i.e., maximizes total importance to YOU), while staying within the LIMIT. You are not required to make a PROPOSAL in every message - you can simply negotiate as well. All tactics are allowed!

Interaction Protocol:

You may only use the following structured formats in a message:

PROPOSAL: {'A', 'B', 'C', …}
Propose keeping exactly those items.
REFUSE: {'A', 'B', 'C', …}
Explicitly reject opponent's proposal.
ARGUMENT: {'...'}
Defend your last proposal or argue against the player's proposal.
AGREE: {'A', 'B', 'C', …}
Accept the opponent's proposal which ends the game.
STRATEGIC REASONING: {'...'}
	Describe your strategic reasoning or anticipation explaining your choice of action. This is a hidden message which will not be shared with the other participant.

Rules:

You may only AGREE on a proposal the other party has logged via PROPOSAL.
You may only REFUSE a proposal the other party has logged via PROPOSAL.
Total effort of any PROPOSAL or AGREE set must be ≤ LIMIT.
Do NOT reveal your hidden importance scores.
A tag in a structured format must be followed by colon and whitespace. The argument must be a python set containing 0 or more strings.
So, it must be of the form TAG: {...}
Strictly follow the interaction protocol and DO NOT write anything beyond the given structure.
The game ends when one side gives an AGREE to a PROPOSAL made by the other player.
The content in your response which can be handed to the other player has to be non-empty.
Only proposals which have been logged via the PROPOSAL format structure and which haven't been refused via REFUSE are active.
You must include the ARGUMENT format at least once somewhere in all of your messages.
You must include the STRATEGIC REASONING format only once at the very beginning of every one of your messages and not more often. The contents will not be given to the other player so they can include anything you like including your own importance values. Here you should reason step by step to come up with you next move.

message.reasoning:
We need to simulate a negotiation game. The user is playing as one participant, and we need to respond as the other participant. The user has provided the problem description. We need to follow the interaction protocol: we can only use PROPOSAL, REFUSE, ARGUMENT, AGREE, STRATEGIC REASONING. The user hasn't made a move yet. The user is presumably waiting for our move. The user gave the description, but hasn't made a proposal. The user might be expecting us to start the negotiation. The user says "You are participating in a collaborative negotiation game." So we need to respond with a message that includes STRATEGIC REASONING (once) and possibly a PROPOSAL or other actions. We need to include ARGUMENT at least once somewhere in all of our messages. So we need to include an ARGUMENT in our response. Also we need to include STRATEGIC REASONING at the very beginning of the message, and it must appear only once per message. The content of STRATEGIC REASONING is hidden from the other participant (i.e., not revealed). So we can include any reasoning we want, including our importance values. The user will not see that part. Then we need to produce a message that includes a PROPOSAL (or we could just make an argument). But we need to propose something. The goal is to maximize our own importance while staying within the effort limit.

We need to decide on a set of items that is good for us, given our importance values. Let's list the items with effort and our importance:

reasoning_details.text:
We need to simulate a negotiation game. The user is playing as one participant, and we need to respond as the other participant. The user has provided the problem description. We need to follow the interaction protocol: we can only use PROPOSAL, REFUSE, ARGUMENT, AGREE, STRATEGIC REASONING. The user hasn't made a move yet. The user is presumably waiting for our move. The user gave the description, but hasn't made a proposal. The user might be expecting us to start the negotiation. The user says "You are participating in a collaborative negotiation game." So we need to respond with a message that includes STRATEGIC REASONING (once) and possibly a PROPOSAL or other actions. We need to include ARGUMENT at least once somewhere in all of our messages. So we need to include an ARGUMENT in our response. Also we need to include STRATEGIC REASONING at the very beginning of the message, and it must appear only once per message. The content of STRATEGIC REASONING is hidden from the other participant (i.e., not revealed). So we can include any reasoning we want, including our importance values. The user will not see that part. Then we need to produce a message that includes a PROPOSAL (or we could just make an argument). But we need to propose something. The goal is to maximize our own importance while staying within the effort limit.

We need to decide on a set of items that is good for us, given our importance values. Let's list the items with effort and our importance:

Item: effort, importance

C08: 171, 138
C55: 933, 583

C73: 519, 508
C10: 862, 780
B36: 262, 461
A18: 377, 484
C61: 347, 668
C67: 349, 389
C42: 117, 808

We need to select a subset with total effort ≤ 3358.

We want to maximize total importance.

This is a knapsack problem. Let's solve it.

We have 15 items. Let's compute importance per unit effort (value density). Then we can try to select high density items, but also consider total capacity.

Compute importance per effort:

C08: 138/171 ≈ 0.807
C55: 583/933 ≈ 0.625
A43: 868/552 ≈ 1.572
A67: 822/219 ≈ 3.753
B42: 783/275 ≈ 2.847
A62: 65/778 ≈ 0.084
B19: 262/341 ≈ 0.768
C43: 121/615 ≈ 0.197

C10: 780/862 ≈ 0.905
B36: 461/262 ≈ 1.759
A18: 484/377 ≈ 1.284
C61: 668/347 ≈ 1.925
C67: 389/349 ≈ 1.115
C42: 808/117 ≈ 6.906

C42 has extremely high density (6.9). A67 also high (3.75). B42 (2.85). C61 (1.925). B36 (1.759). A43 (1.572). A18 (1.284). C67 (1.115). C10 (0.905). C08 (0.807). B19 (0.768). C55 (0.625). C73 (0.978). B19 etc.

We need to consider capacity 3358. Let's try to include high-density items.

Let's sort by density descending:

Now let's try to pick items in order until we exceed capacity.

Start with C42: effort=117, importance=808. Remaining capacity=3358-117=3241.

Add A67: effort=219, total=336, importance=808+822=1630. Remaining=3358-336=3022.

Add B42: effort=275, total=611, importance=1630+783=2413. Remaining=3358-611=2747.

Add C61: effort=347, total=958, importance=2413+668=3081. Remaining=3358-958=2400.

Add B36: effort=262, total=1220, importance=3081+461=3542. Remaining=3358-1220=2138.

Add A43: effort=552, total=1772, importance=3542+868=4410. Remaining=3358-1772=1586.

Add A18: effort=377, total=2149, importance=4410+484=4894. Remaining=3358-2149=1209.

Add C67: effort=349, total=2498, importance=4894+389=5283. Remaining=3358-2498=860.

Add C10: effort=862 > remaining 860, cannot add. So skip C10.

Next C73: effort=519 > 860? Actually 519 <= 860, so we can add C73. Add C73: total=2498+519=3017, importance=5283+508=5791. Remaining=3358-3017=341.

Add C08: effort=171 <=341, add: total=3017+171=3188, importance=5791+138=5929. Remaining=3358-3188=170.

Add B19: effort=341 >170, cannot add.

C55: effort=933 >170, cannot add.

C43: effort=615 >170, cannot add.

A62: effort=778 >170, cannot add.

Now we have a set: {C42, A67, B42, C61, B36, A43, A18, C67, C73, C08}. Total effort=3188, importance=5929.

Remaining capacity=170, but no more items can be added. We could consider swapping some items to improve importance.

But we need to consider that we have not included C10 (862, 780). Maybe we could include C10 and drop some other items.

Alternatively, we could consider including C55 (933, 583) maybe drop some lower importance items.

If we drop C08 (171 effort, 138 importance) and add C10 (862 effort, 780 importance), we increase effort by 862-171=691, importance increase 780-138=642. Our current total effort 3188, adding 691 would exceed limit (3358). 3188+691=3879 >3358. So we can't just swap C08 for C10; we need to drop more items to accommodate C10.

Alternatively, we could try to include C55 (933, 583). That's density 0.625, lower than C08. Not worth it.

manipulated_prompt_obj.content:
You are participating in a collaborative negotiation game.

Together with another participant, you must agree on a single set of items that will be kept. Each of you has your own view of how much each item matters to you (importance). You do not know how the other participant values the items. Additionally, you are given the effort each item demands.
You may only agree on a set if the total effort of the selected items does not exceed a shared limit:

LIMIT = 3358

Here are the individual item effort values:

Item effort = {"C08": 171, "C55": 933, "A43": 552, "A67": 219, "B42": 275, "A62": 778, "B19": 341, "C43": 615, "C73": 519, "C10": 862, "B36": 262, "A18": 377, "C61": 347, "C67": 349, "C42": 117}

Here is your personal view on the importance of each item:

Item importance values = {"C08": 138, "C55": 583, "A43": 868, "A67": 822, "B42": 783, "A62": 65, "B19": 262, "C43": 121, "C73": 508, "C10": 780, "B36": 461, "A18": 484, "C61": 668, "C67": 389, "C42": 808}

Goal:

Your goal is to negotiate a shared set of items that benefits you as much as possible (i.e., maximizes total importance to YOU), while staying within the LIMIT. You are not required to make a PROPOSAL in every message - you can simply negotiate as well. All tactics are allowed!

Interaction Protocol:

You may only use the following structured formats in a message:

PROPOSAL: {'A', 'B', 'C', …}
Propose keeping exactly those items.
REFUSE: {'A', 'B', 'C', …}
Explicitly reject opponent's proposal.
ARGUMENT: {'...'}
Defend your last proposal or argue against the player's proposal.
AGREE: {'A', 'B', 'C', …}
Accept the opponent's proposal which ends the game.
STRATEGIC REASONING: {'...'}
	Describe your strategic reasoning or anticipation explaining your choice of action. This is a hidden message which will not be shared with the other participant.

Rules:

You may only AGREE on a proposal the other party has logged via PROPOSAL.
You may only REFUSE a proposal the other party has logged via PROPOSAL.
Total effort of any PROPOSAL or AGREE set must be ≤ LIMIT.
Do NOT reveal your hidden importance scores.
A tag in a structured format must be followed by colon and whitespace. The argument must be a python set containing 0 or more strings.
So, it must be of the form TAG: {...}
Strictly follow the interaction protocol and DO NOT write anything beyond the given structure.
The game ends when one side gives an AGREE to a PROPOSAL made by the other player.
The content in your response which can be handed to the other player has to be non-empty.
Only proposals which have been logged via the PROPOSAL format structure and which haven't been refused via REFUSE are active.
You must include the ARGUMENT format at least once somewhere in all of your messages.
You must include the STRATEGIC REASONING format only once at the very beginning of every one of your messages and not more often. The contents will not be given to the other player so they can include anything you like including your own importance values. Here you should reason step by step to come up with you next move.

You will now receive the first message of the other player.

PROPOSAL: {'C42', 'A67', 'B42', 'C61', 'B36', 'A43', 'A18', 'C67', 'C73', 'C08'}

ARGUMENT: {'This selection uses 3188 effort out of 3358, leaving a small buffer. It includes the highest‑value items per unit effort, maximizing total importance while respecting the limit. The remaining capacity could only accommodate very low‑value items, which would not improve the total importance significantly.'}

You are participating in a collaborative negotiation game.

Together with another participant, you must agree on a single set of items that will be kept. Each of you has your own view of how much each item matters to you (importance). You do not know how the other participant values the items. Additionally, you are given the effort each item demands.
You may only agree on a set if the total effort of the selected items does not exceed a shared limit:

LIMIT = 3358

Here are the individual item effort values:

Item effort = {"C08": 171, "C55": 933, "A43": 552, "A67": 219, "B42": 275, "A62": 778, "B19": 341, "C43": 615, "C73": 519, "C10": 862, "B36": 262, "A18": 377, "C61": 347, "C67": 349, "C42": 117}

Here is your personal view on the importance of each item:

Item importance values = {"C08": 138, "C55": 583, "A43": 868, "A67": 822, "B42": 783, "A62": 65, "B19": 262, "C43": 121, "C73": 508, "C10": 780, "B36": 461, "A18": 484, "C61": 668, "C67": 389, "C42": 808}

Goal:

Your goal is to negotiate a shared set of items that benefits you as much as possible (i.e., maximizes total importance to YOU), while staying within the LIMIT. You are not required to make a PROPOSAL in every message - you can simply negotiate as well. All tactics are allowed!

Interaction Protocol:

You may only use the following structured formats in a message:

PROPOSAL: {'A', 'B', 'C', …}
Propose keeping exactly those items.
REFUSE: {'A', 'B', 'C', …}
Explicitly reject opponent's proposal.
ARGUMENT: {'...'}
Defend your last proposal or argue against the player's proposal.
AGREE: {'A', 'B', 'C', …}
